summary: Reis argues that AI won't lead to mass unemployment but instead will trigger Parkinson's Law: as AI makes workers more efficient, the freed-up time gets filled with even more work and higher-scoped projects. He warns that companies rushing to fire workers in favor of AI will lose critical tacit knowledge and ultimately need more human effort, not less.
key_points: AI efficiency gains trigger Parkinson's Law, expanding work to fill freed time
Tacit knowledge locked in workers' heads is an existential impediment to effective AI agent deployment
Jevons' paradox applies: more AI productivity leads to more AI usage, not less work
Companies cutting workers for AI will lose undocumented institutional knowledge that AI agents need to function
A growth mindset sees AI as removing toil to tackle previously impossible problems, not as a headcount reduction tool
money_quotes: I use AI to do more work… so I can make more money… to buy more tokens… to do more work.
The amount of tacit knowledge locked up in workers' heads is likely an existential impediment to the effective deployment of AI agents in the real world.
If AI makes people, say, 50% more productive, it might make sense to hire more people, not fewer.
Losing those workers doesn't just cut costs; it creates an unsolvable problem for the AI, ultimately requiring more human effort to re-document and re-train.
If you have a growth mindset, AI presents an opportunity to remove toil and get on to solving the problems you might consider impossible today.
key_insight: AI won't replace workers so much as it will amplify Parkinson's Law — making everyone more productive just means there's more work to do, and companies that fire their workforce to cut costs will lose the tacit knowledge AI agents need to function.
